title: Salary statistics by department
instruction:
Aggregate functions summarize many rows into single numbers. `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`, and `COUNT` are the most common.

Get salary statistics per department — this is a real HR reporting query.
starter:
SELECT
  d.name                           AS department,
  COUNT(e.id)                      AS headcount,
  MIN(e.salary)                    AS min_salary,
  MAX(e.salary)                    AS max_salary,
  ROUND(AVG(e.salary))             AS avg_salary
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
GROUP BY d.name
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;
successMessage: Data Science has only 2 people but the highest average salary ($166,500). Engineering has 5 people but a wider range ($92k–$185k). This kind of report helps HR spot pay equity issues and budget gaps.

title: Revenue analysis — SUM with filters
instruction:
`SUM` adds up numeric values. Combined with `WHERE` or `HAVING`, you can build revenue dashboards.

Find total revenue by product type, but only show product categories with over $20k in revenue.
starter:
SELECT
  product,
  COUNT(*)                         AS order_count,
  ROUND(SUM(amount))               AS total_revenue,
  ROUND(AVG(amount))               AS avg_order_value
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'paid'
GROUP BY product
HAVING SUM(amount) > 20000
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;
successMessage: Enterprise Plan drives $192k of revenue — by far the biggest. `HAVING SUM(amount) > 20000` filters after aggregation (unlike `WHERE` which filters before). The Support Add-on falls below $20k so it's excluded.

title: HAVING vs WHERE — know the difference
instruction:
`WHERE` filters rows **before** grouping. `HAVING` filters groups **after** aggregation. Mixing them up is one of the most common SQL mistakes.

Find departments where the average salary is above $120k AND have more than 2 employees.
starter:
SELECT
  d.name                       AS department,
  COUNT(e.id)                  AS headcount,
  ROUND(AVG(e.salary))         AS avg_salary,
  SUM(e.salary)                AS total_payroll
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
GROUP BY d.name
HAVING COUNT(e.id) > 2 AND AVG(e.salary) > 120000
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;
successMessage: Only Engineering passes both filters: more than 2 people AND average salary above $120k. Marketing has 3 people but its average is only $118k — it's filtered out by `HAVING AVG(e.salary) > 120000`.
